title: Highest temperature in London on July 21?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 21 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from the Met Office and BBC project London highs of 25–26°C on July 21 under mostly sunny conditions with light northerly winds and minimal cloud cover. This narrow range underpins the closely matched market probabilities for 24–26°C outcomes, reflecting ensemble model spread in peak timing and boundary-layer warming rates. Primary drivers include persistent high pressure favoring clear skies and modest warm advection, tempered by uncertainty in exact diurnal maxima across guidance. Updated runs from major numerical weather prediction centers over the next 48 hours will further constrain resolution criteria based on official observational maxima.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent ensemble forecasts from Météo-France and ECMWF place Paris’s July 21 maximum near 26°C under partly sunny conditions following the early-July heatwave. Small differences in afternoon cloud timing, boundary-layer mixing, and weak southerly flow create a tight distribution across the 25–27°C range, explaining the closely matched market-implied odds. Historical July maxima average 24–25°C, so current guidance represents a modest positive anomaly with limited upside risk from stronger subsidence or clearer skies. Updated high-resolution runs and the next 12z cycle will refine the exact peak before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent KMA observations and medium-range guidance indicate Seoul will remain under a persistent high-pressure pattern with daytime highs several degrees above the July climatological average of ~29°C, supporting the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 27–29°C. Model consensus points to light winds, elevated humidity, and modest cloud cover or scattered showers that could cap the daily maximum near 28–29°C, while any clearing or delayed precipitation raises the chance of reaching 30°C or higher. The 1–2°C spread among leading outcomes reflects typical forecast uncertainty in peak temperature timing and local urban heat-island effects two days ahead of resolution, with updated short-range model runs from KMA and global ensembles expected to narrow the range before July 21.

eventMetadata.context_description: Persistent high-pressure systems and clear, dry conditions across central Anatolia are positioning Ankara for a July 21 daily maximum near 33–34 °C, aligning with the market’s heaviest weighting on those outcomes. Numerical weather models from the Turkish State Meteorological Service and ECMWF consensus runs show light northerly flow, minimal cloud cover, and surface heating typical of the region’s continental climate, where July averages reach about 32 °C. Recent observations confirm stable subsidence and negligible moisture advection that would otherwise limit daytime warming or trigger convective cooling. With the event only two days away and no significant model shifts anticipated before resolution, trader sentiment remains anchored to these narrow temperature bands rather than outliers driven by atypical synoptic changes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Short-range ensemble forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts and global models currently anchor trader sentiment around a Munich high of 22–23 °C for July 21, with 23 °C holding the narrow lead at 35.5 % implied probability. Light northwesterly flow, limited cloud cover, and typical mid-July radiative heating support modest daytime warming without strong advection or convective cooling. Recent model runs show tight clustering between 21–24 °C, reflecting low uncertainty in steering patterns and boundary-layer moisture over southern Bavaria. A modest trough passage or unexpected increase in low-level cloud could trim the peak by 1–2 °C, while clearer skies and slightly drier air would favor the upper end of that range. Updated 48-hour guidance expected later today will likely determine whether the 22 °C or 23 °C bracket consolidates further.

eventMetadata.context_description: Singapore’s equatorial location and Southwest Monsoon conditions keep daily highs tightly clustered near 30–32°C, with afternoon thundery showers frequently capping temperatures through increased cloud cover and evaporative cooling. Current forecasts from regional models show modest variability around these values for July 21, influenced by the timing and coverage of convection as well as lingering El Niño-driven warmth that has elevated baseline readings this season. The near-even market odds between 30°C and 32°C reflect this narrow uncertainty band, where small shifts in shower onset or wind patterns could determine whether the official observation peaks at the lower or upper end of the range. Historical July maxima average near 31°C, underscoring how routine monsoon dynamics dominate over longer-term trends for this short-term resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models from sources like timeanddate and AccuWeather project Beijing's July 21 high in the 29–31°C range, driven by typical mid-July East Asian monsoon conditions that bring humid air masses, scattered thunderstorms, and variable cloud cover capable of limiting peak solar heating. These factors create tight differentiation among the leading market outcomes, as even modest shifts in precipitation timing, wind speed, or boundary-layer moisture can alter afternoon maxima by 1–2°C through evaporative cooling or enhanced greenhouse warming. With the market-implied probabilities clustered around 30–32°C and no extreme heat signals in recent model runs, traders are weighting the historical July average of roughly 31°C against the cooling potential of ongoing convective activity expected through midweek. Updated ensemble guidance and official China Meteorological Administration briefings over the next 48 hours will likely refine these probabilities as new observational data arrives.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent model runs from major forecasting centers show Moscow temperatures on July 21 likely peaking near 22–24 °C, with ensemble spreads reflecting modest uncertainty in cloud cover, boundary-layer mixing, and weak high-pressure ridging over European Russia. Updated runs from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts and Global Forecast System will likely refine the outlook before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent model consensus from agencies like the South African Weather Service points to a mild winter high near 16–17°C for Cape Town on July 21, driven by a strengthening South Atlantic high-pressure system and the stabilizing influence of the cold Benguela Current, which typically caps daytime maxima in the mid-teens during July. Light northwesterly winds and limited cloud cover are expected to support modest warming, yet subtle differences in steering patterns or a passing weak front could shift the peak by 1°C. Historical July averages hover around 18°C, but current ensemble guidance shows tight clustering that explains why traders assign the highest implied probabilities to 16°C and 17°C outcomes while leaving room for minor revisions as new runs arrive.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts for Guangzhou point to scattered thunderstorms and persistent monsoon moisture around July 21, tempering peak heating and keeping daily highs near the 31–33 °C range that dominates trader positioning. The South China Sea summer monsoon supplies humid southerly flow that fuels afternoon convection, increasing cloud cover and reducing surface insolation while urban heat-island effects in the Pearl River Delta sustain baseline warmth. Model spreads arise mainly from differences in predicted cloud timing and rainfall intensity, with heavier showers favoring the lower end near 31 °C and clearer intervals supporting 33 °C. Historical July climatology shows mean highs of 32–33 °C, but day-to-day convective variability routinely produces 2–3 °C swings. Updated guidance from regional meteorological agencies will refine these thresholds before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Monsoon-driven cloud cover and intermittent rain or thundershowers remain the dominant influences on Lucknow’s maximum temperature for July 21, keeping trader-implied odds tightly clustered around 30–32 °C. Recent India Meteorological Department guidance notes overcast skies with isolated heavy showers and thunderstorms across Uttar Pradesh, which reduce incoming solar radiation and limit afternoon heating relative to clearer pre-monsoon conditions. Historical July averages hover near 32 °C, yet active monsoon surges frequently suppress daily highs by 2–4 °C through increased humidity, evaporative cooling, and reduced insolation. The narrow spread between the 31 °C and 30 °C outcomes reflects uncertainty in rainfall timing and intensity: lighter or delayed showers could allow brief clearing and a 1–2 °C rise, while persistent convection would cap readings near or below 30 °C. Updated model runs and official forecasts over the next 48 hours will likely sharpen these distinctions ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current meteorological forecasts from INMET and ensemble models position 28°C as the leading outcome for São Paulo’s July 21 high, with traders assigning it 48% implied probability amid stable winter conditions in the Southern Hemisphere. Recent model runs indicate a ridge of high pressure promoting clear skies and moderate northerly flow, supporting daytime maxima near the climatological upper range of 27–29°C rather than cooler anomalies typical of July. Ensemble consensus shows limited spread, with 27°C and 29°C as secondary possibilities at 29.5% and 12%, while probabilities drop sharply outside this band due to minimal expected variability in steering patterns or moisture advection. Updated guidance ahead of the 24-hour verification window will determine final resolution against official surface observations.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models for Buenos Aires on July 21 indicate a likely daily maximum near 13–14 °C under a cool, moist airmass with light northerly flow and possible showers, consistent with mid-winter climatology where average highs reach about 15 °C. The market’s close spread across 13–15 °C outcomes reflects remaining uncertainty in exact timing of any brief clearing or wind shift that could add or subtract 1–2 °C, as well as model differences in boundary-layer mixing and cloud cover. Official guidance from regional meteorological services will resolve the market once the maximum is recorded.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent model consensus from agencies like Environment Canada points to moderate high pressure and limited cloud cover over southern Ontario, supporting a Toronto high near the July climatological average of 26°C on July 21, which explains its leading 26% market-implied probability. Subtle variations in forecast guidance around lake-breeze effects from Lake Ontario, boundary-layer mixing, and any weak shortwave troughs create the tight clustering between 24–28°C outcomes, with traders weighting ensemble spreads that show only modest day-to-day warming potential. Urban heat-island amplification and dew-point trends add further uncertainty within this narrow range, while probabilities drop sharply above 29°C absent stronger subsidence or southerly flow. Updated model runs expected overnight will likely refine these distinctions ahead of resolution.

Atlanta’s subtropical July climate features average highs near 89°F, but recent days have trended slightly above normal amid humid, weakly sheared flow. No major forecast shifts have occurred in the past 48 hours, supporting the market’s concentration around the 90–93°F range.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Forecast models and recent observations position 92-93°F as the most probable high for Miami on July 21, 2026, aligning with the market’s strong 69.5% implied probability.** Typical mid-July conditions feature daytime highs near the long-term average of 90°F, rarely exceeding 92°F without a strong high-pressure ridge or suppressed sea breeze. Current National Weather Service guidance and ensemble runs indicate partly cloudy skies with a modest onshore flow and isolated afternoon showers, which historically limit peak temperatures to the low 90s rather than pushing into the mid-90s.

Slightly cooler 90-91°F outcomes carry 17.5% odds, reflecting scenarios with earlier or stronger convective activity that caps heating. The 94-95°F bin at 16.0% remains plausible if steering patterns allow greater subsidence and reduced cloud cover, but recent model consensus has not shifted strongly in that direction. Higher thresholds above 96°F show minimal probability (<5% combined) given the absence of anomalous heat signals and Miami’s climatological July maximum rarely surpassing 92-93°F outside exceptional events.

Traders appear to be weighting the latest short-range guidance and historical analogs, where typical summer patterns produce highs clustered around 90-93°F. Updated model runs or shifts in tropical moisture ahead of July 21 could still adjust the distribution, particularly if convective timing or wind direction changes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current National Weather Service and ensemble model guidance indicates a seasonable high near 86°F for Chicago on July 21, driven by moderate high pressure, limited warm advection, and dew points in the mid-60s that constrain daytime heating close to the 84.6°F July normal. Trader consensus clusters around the 84–87°F bins because small differences in boundary-layer mixing, cloud timing, and steering flow can shift the daily maximum by a degree or two across model runs. Recent cooler readings after mid-July heat episodes have kept probabilities for 88°F+ modest while capping downside risk below 82°F absent stronger northerly flow. Updated short-range guidance tonight will likely refine these narrow ranges ahead of official resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus centers on 98–99°F as the most probable high for Houston on July 21 because short-range forecast guidance from models such as the GFS and NAM indicates surface temperatures peaking slightly above the 95°F climatological normal amid light winds and abundant Gulf moisture. Afternoon convective cloud development and a possible weak sea-breeze front introduce the main uncertainty, potentially capping readings in the mid-90s or allowing brief clearing that supports upper-90s values. Historical analogs for late-July patterns show that modest variations in boundary-layer mixing or timing of diurnal heating can easily shift the daily maximum by 3–5°F, explaining why the adjacent 96–97°F and 100–101°F bins also carry substantial implied probability while lower and higher extremes remain discounted.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest National Weather Service and ensemble model guidance point to a marine-layer moderated high of 80–82°F in Los Angeles on July 21, establishing the primary driver behind the market’s concentration of implied probability on the 80–81°F bin (43.5%). Persistent onshore flow and low-level stratus have kept coastal maxima several degrees below the broader summer 2026 above-normal temperature outlook issued by NOAA’s Climate Prediction Center, while recent 48-hour runs show limited warming potential before the next diurnal cycle. Historical July climatology for downtown and LAX stations places the 80–83°F range as the most frequent outcome under similar synoptic patterns, reinforcing trader consensus reflected in the combined 98.5% probability assigned to 78–83°F. Updated short-range forecasts tomorrow morning will provide the final inputs ahead of official station observations used for resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Marine layer influence and typical July climatology position 81°F or below as the dominant outcome at 64.5% implied probability.** San Francisco’s coastal setting produces persistent onshore flow of cool Pacific air, reinforced by the marine layer—a shallow stratus deck that forms overnight and often lingers into the afternoon, capping daytime highs near the seasonal average of 66–70°F. Current forecasts for July 21 show highs in the low-to-mid 60s to low 70s under continued westerly flow and no significant offshore wind shift, keeping temperatures well below the 81°F threshold.  

The next two buckets (82–83°F at 23.5% and 84–85°F at 7.5%) reflect residual uncertainty around marine-layer clearance timing or a brief warming pulse, but model consensus and historical July records (rarely exceeding 79°F) limit those probabilities. Higher bins remain thin because sustained heat requires strong high-pressure ridging and easterly flow—neither of which is indicated in the latest guidance. Traders are therefore pricing in the high likelihood of another cool, fog-influenced day consistent with long-term patterns.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from sources like AccuWeather and timeanddate.com indicate Mexico City highs near 25°C on July 21 amid the rainy season, with afternoon showers likely moderating peak readings at the city's high elevation. Typical July conditions feature afternoon convection and cloud cover that caps temperatures around the 24–26°C range, consistent with climatological baselines. Trader consensus favoring 26–27°C brackets likely reflects recent observed variability, including daily maxima reaching 27–28°C earlier in the month, plus uncertainty in precise model runs for boundary-layer heating and timing of precipitation. Official monitoring continues to emphasize the influence of subtropical moisture and orographic effects on daily maxima.

markets.description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Marcos A. Gelabert Intl Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 21 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Marcos A. Gelabert Intl Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/pa/panama-city/MPMG.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader sentiment for Panama City’s July 21 high temperature centers on the narrow 30–31 °C band (combined ~58 % implied probability), consistent with long-term July climatology of roughly 30 °C average daily maxima.**  

In Panama’s Pacific coastal zone during the height of the rainy season, persistent cloud cover, high atmospheric moisture, and frequent afternoon convection tied to the Intertropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) act as the dominant cooling mechanisms. These factors typically cap daytime heating near 30–31 °C by reducing incoming solar radiation and triggering evaporative cooling from showers. Model guidance and recent analogs show only modest day-to-day variability (±1–2 °C) under prevailing trade-wind and sea-breeze regimes, which explains why outcomes outside 29–32 °C carry sharply lower probabilities.  

Slightly higher odds on 31 °C versus 30 °C reflect the possibility of marginally clearer morning skies or a delayed shower onset allowing brief additional insolation, while 32 °C+ remains a low-probability outlier requiring unusually suppressed convection. Official station measurements (typically Tocumen or Albrook) incorporate these local effects, and traders weigh the latest short-range model consensus on cloud thickness and precipitation timing as the key variables that will resolve the market.
